title: What Matters for On-Policy Deep Actor-Critic Methods? A Large-Scale Study

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - WHAT MATTERS FOR ON-POLICY DEEP ACTOR CRITIC METHODS? A LARGE-SCALE STUDY

**Marcin Andrychowicz, Anton Raichuk, Piotr Sta´nczyk, Manu Orsini, Sertan Girgin, Raphaël Marinier, Léonard Hussenot, Matthieu Geist, Olivier Pietquin, Marcin Michalski, Sylvain Gelly, Olivier Bachem**

Google Research, Brain Team

## ABSTRACT

In recent years, reinforcement learning (RL) has been successfully applied to many different continuous control tasks. While RL algorithms are often conceptually simple, their state-of-the-art implementations take numerous low- and high-level design decisions that strongly affect the performance of the resulting agents. Those choices are usually not extensively discussed in the literature, leading to discrepancy between published descriptions of algorithms and their implementations. This makes it hard to attribute progress in RL and slows down overall progress [27]. As a step towards filling that gap, we implement >50 such “choices” in a unified on-policy deep actor-critic framework, allowing us to investigate their impact in a large-scale empirical study. We train over 250’000 agents in five continuous control environments of different complexity and provide insights and practical recommendations for the training of on-policy deep actor-critic RL agents.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has seen increased interest in recent years due to its ability to have neural-network-based agents learn to act in environments through interactions. For continuous control tasks, on-policy algorithms such as REINFORCE [2], TRPO [10], A3C [14], PPO [17] and off-policy algorithms such as DDPG [13] and SAC [21] have enabled successful applications such as quadrupedal locomotion [20], self-driving [30] or dexterous in-hand manipulation [20, 25, 32].

Many of these papers investigate in depth different algorithmic ideas, for example different loss functions and learning paradigms. Yet, it is less visible that behind successful experiments in deep RL there are complicated code bases that contain a large number of low- and high-level design decisions that are usually not discussed in research papers. While one may assume that such “choices” do not matter, there is evidence that they are in fact crucial for or even driving good performance [27].

While there are open-source implementations available that can be used by practitioners, this is still unsatisfactory: Research publications often contain one-to-one comparisons of different algorithmic ideas based on implementations in different code bases. This makes it impossible to assess whether improvements are due to the underlying algorithmic idea or due to the implementation. In fact, it is hard to assess the performance of high-level algorithmic ideas without an understanding of lower-level choices as performance may strongly depend on the tuning of hyperparameters and implementationlevel details. Overall, this makes it hard to attribute progress in reinforcement learning and slows down further research [15, 22, 27].

**Our contributions.** Our key goal in this paper is to investigate such lower level choices in depth and to understand their impact on final agent performance. Hence, as our key contributions, we (1) implement >50 choices in a unified on-policy deep actor-critic implementation[1] , (2) conducted a large-scale (more than 250’000 agents trained) experimental study that covers different aspects of the training process, and (3) analyze the experimental results to provide practical insights and recommendations for the training of on-policy deep actor-critic RL agents.

> 1 The implementation is available at https://github.com/google-research/seed_rl.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Most surprising finding.** While many of our experimental findings confirm common RL practices, some of them are quite surprising, e.g. the policy initialization scheme significantly influences the performance while it is rarely even mentioned in RL publications. In particular, we have found that initializing the network so that the initial action distribution has zero mean, a rather low standard deviation and is independent of the observation significantly improves the training speed (Sec. 3.2).

**Paper outline.** The rest of of this paper is structured as follows: We describe our experimental setup and performance metrics used in Sec. 2. Then, in Sec. 3 we present and analyse the experimental results and finish with related work in Sec. 4 and conclusions in Sec. 5. The appendices contain the detailed description of all design choices we experiment with (App. B), default hyperparameters (App. C) and the raw experimental results (App. D - K).

## 2 STUDY DESIGN

**Considered setting.** In this paper, we consider the setting of _on-policy deep actor-critic reinforcement learning for continuous control_ . We define on-policy learning in the following loose sense: We consider policy iteration algorithms that iterate between generating experience using the current policy and using that experience to improve the policy. This is the standard _modus operandi_ of algorithms usually considered on-policy such as PPO [17]. However, we note that algorithms often perform several model updates and thus may operate technically on off-policy data within a single policy improvement iteration. As benchmark environments, we consider five widely used continuous control environments from OpenAI Gym [12] of varying complexity: Hopper-v1, Walker2d-v1, HalfCheetah-v1, Ant-v1, and Humanoid-v1[2] .

**Unified on-policy deep actor-critic gradient algorithm.** We took the following approach to create a highly configurable unified on-policy deep actor-critic gradient algorithm with as many choices as possible:

1. We researched prior work and popular code bases to make a list of commonly used choices, i.e., different loss functions (both for value functions and policies), architectural choices such as initialization methods, heuristic tricks such as gradient clipping and all their corresponding hyperparameters.

2. Based on this, we implemented a single, unified on-policy deep actor-critic agent and corresponding training protocol starting from the SEED RL code base [28]. Whenever we were faced with implementation decisions that required us to take decisions that could not be clearly motivated or had alternative solutions, we further added such decisions as additional choices.

3. We verified that when all choices are selected as in the PPO implementation from OpenAI baselines, we obtain similar performance as reported in the PPO paper [17]. We chose PPO because it is probably the most commonly used on-policy deep actor-critic RL algorithm at the moment.

The resulting agent implementation is detailed in Appendix B. The key property is that the implementation exposes all choices as configuration options in an unified manner. For convenience, we mark each of the choice in this paper with a number (e.g., C1) and a fixed name (e.g. num_envs (C1)) that can be easily used to find a description of the choice in Appendix B.

**Difficulty of investigating choices.** The primary goal of this paper is to understand how the different choices affect the final performance of an agent and to derive recommendations for these choices. There are two key reasons why this is challenging:

First, we are mainly interested in insights on choices for good hyperparameter configurations. Yet, if all choices are sampled randomly, the performance is very bad and little (if any) training progress is made. This may be explained by the presence of sub-optimal settings (e.g., hyperparameters of the wrong scale) that prohibit learning at all. If there are many choices, the probability of such failure increases exponentially.

> 2It has been noticed that the version of the Mujoco physics simulator [5] can slightly influence the behaviour of some of the environments — https://github.com/openai/gym/issues/1541. We used Mujoco 2.0 in our experiments.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Second, many choices may have strong interactions with other related choices, for example the learning rate and the minibatch size. This means that such choices need to be tuned together and experiments where only a single choice is varied but interacting choices are kept fixed may lead to misleading conclusions.

**Basic experimental design.** To address these issues, we design a series of experiments as follows: We create groups of choices around thematic groups where we suspect interactions between different choices, for example we group together all choices related to neural network architecture. We also include Adam learning rate (C24) in all of the groups as we suspect that it may interact with many other choices.

Then, in each experiment, we train a large number of models where we randomly sample the choices within the corresponding group[3] . All other settings (for choices not in the group) are set to settings of a competitive base configuration (detailed in Appendix C) that is close to the default PPOv2 configuration[4] scaled up to 256 parallel environments. This has two effects: First, it ensures that our set of trained models contains good models (as verified by performance statistics in the corresponding results). Second, it guarantees that we have models that have different combinations of potentially interacting choices.

We consider two different analyses for each choice (e.g, for advantage_estimator (C6)):

_Conditional 95th percentile_ : For each potential value of that choice (e.g., advantage_estimator (C6) = N-Step), we look at the performance distribution of sampled configurations with that value. We report the 95th percentile of the performance as well as a confidence interval based on a binomial approximation[5] . Intuitively, this corresponds to a robust estimate of the performance one can expect if all other choices in the group were tuned with random search and a limited budget of roughly 20 hyperparameter configurations.

_Distribution of choice within top 5% configurations._ We further consider for each choice the distribution of values among the top 5% configurations trained in that experiment. The reasoning is as follows: By design of the experiment, values for each choice are distributed uniformly at random. Thus, if certain values are over-represented in the top models, this indicates that the specific choice is important in guaranteeing good performance.

**Performance measures.** We employ the following way to compute performance: For each choice configuration, we train 3 models with independent random seeds where each model is trained for one million (Hopper, HalfCheetah, Walker2d) or two million environment steps (Ant, Humanoid). We evaluate trained policies every hundred thousand steps by freezing the policy and computing the average undiscounted episode return of 100 episodes (with the stochastic policy). We then average these score to obtain a single performance score of the seed which is proportional to the area under the learning curve. This ensures we assign higher scores to agents that learn quickly. The performance score of a hyperparameter configuration is finally set to the median performance score across the 3 seeds. This reduces the impact of training noise, i.e., that certain seeds of the same configuration may train much better than others.

**Robustness of results.** While we take 3 random seeds to compute the performance measure for a single choice configuration, it is important to note that all the experimental results reported in this paper are based on more than 3 random seeds: The reported _conditional 95th percentile_ and _distribution of choice within top 5% configurations_ are computed based upon the performance of hundreds of choice configurations. Furthermore, we also report confidence intervals for the _conditional 95th percentile_ .

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

We run experiments for eight thematic groups: _Policy Losses_ (Sec. 3.1), _Networks architecture_ (Sec. 3.2), _Normalization and clipping_ (Sec. 3.3), _Advantage Estimation_ (Sec. 3.4), _Training setup_ (Sec. 3.5), _Timesteps handling_ (Sec. 3.6), _Optimizers_ (Sec. 3.7), and _Regularization_ (Sec. 3.8). For each group, we provide a full experimental design and full experimental plots in Appendices D - K so that the reader can draw their own conclusions from the experimental results. Moreover, the raw data from all training runs and a script used to generate all plots for this paper can be found online[6] . In the following sections, we provide short descriptions of the experiments, our interpretation of the results, as well as practical recommendations for agent training for continuous control.

## 3.1 POLICY LOSSES (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX D)

**Study description.** We investigate different policy losses (C14): vanilla policy gradient (PG), V- trace [19], PPO [17], AWR [33], V-MPO[7] [34] and the limiting case of AWR ( _β →_ 0) and V-MPO ( _ϵn →_ 0) which we call Repeat Positive Advantages (RPA) as it is equivalent to the negative logprobability of actions with positive advantages. See App. B.3 for a detailed description of the different losses. We further sweep the hyperparameters of each of the losses (C15, C16, C18, C17, C19), the learning rate (C24) and the number of passes over the data (C3).

The goal of this study is to better understand the importance of the policy loss function in the on-policy deep actor-critic setting considered in this paper. The goal is **not** to provide a general statement that one of the losses is better than the others as some of them were specifically designed for other settings (e.g., the V-trace loss is targeted at near-on-policy data in a distributed setting).

Figure 1: Comparison of different policy losses (C14).

**Interpretation.** Fig. 1 shows the 95-th percentile of the average policy score during training for different policy losses (C14). We observe that PPO performs better than the other losses on 4 out of 5 environments and is one of the top performing losses on HalfCheetah. As we randomly sample the loss specific hyperparameters in this analysis, one might argue that our approach favours choices that are not too sensitive to hyperparameters. At the same time, there might be losses that are sensitive to their hyperparameters but for which good settings may be easily found. Fig. 5 shows that even if we condition on choosing the optimal loss hyperparameters for each loss[8] , PPO still outperforms the other losses on the two hardest tasks — Humanoid and Ant[9] and is one of the top performing losses on the other 3 tasks. Moreover, we show the empirical cumulative density functions of agent performance conditioned on the policy loss used in Fig. 4.
Perhaps unsurprisingly, PG and V-trace perform worse on all tasks. This is likely caused by their inability to handle data that becomes off-policy in one iteration, either due to multiple passes (C3)

While V-Trace contains an off-policy correction, it was designed for ”slightly” off-policy experience arising in ansynchronous, distributed setups and becomes more and more biased as experience becomes more off-policy. Overall, these results show that trust-region optimization (preventing the current policy from diverging too much from the behavioral one) which is present in all the other policy losses is crucial for good sample complexity.

For PPO and its clipping threshold _ϵ_ (C16), we further observe that _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 2 and _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 perform reasonably well in all environments but that lower ( _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 1) or higher ( _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 5) values give better performance on some of the environments (See Fig. 10 and Fig. 32).
**Recommendation.** Use the PPO policy loss. Start with the clipping threshold set to 0 _._ 25 but also try lower and higher values if possible.

## 3.2 NETWORKS ARCHITECTURE (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX E)

**Study description.** We investigate the impact of differences in the policy and value function neural network architectures. We consider choices related to the network structure and size (C47, C48, C49, C50, C51, C52, C52), activation functions (C55), and initialization of network weights (C56, C57, C58). We further include choices related to the standard deviation of actions (C59, C60, C61, C62) and transformations of sampled actions (C63).

**Interpretation.** Separate value and policy networks (C47) appear to lead to better performance on four out of five environments (Fig. 15). To avoid analyzing the other choices based on bad models, we thus focus for the rest of this experiment only on agents with separate value and policy networks. Regarding network sizes, the optimal width of the policy MLP depends on the complexity of the environment (Fig. 18) and too low or too high values can cause significant drop in performance while for the value function there seems to be no downside in using wider networks (Fig. 21). Moreover, on some environments it is beneficial to make the value network wider than the policy one, e.g. on HalfCheetah the best results are achieved with 16 _−_ 32 units per layer in the policy network and 256 in the value network. Two hidden layers appear to work well for policy (Fig. 22) and value networks (Fig. 20) in all tested environments. As for activation functions, we observe that tanh activations perform best and relu worst. (Fig. 30).

Interestingly, the initial policy appears to have a surprisingly high impact on the training performance. The key recipe is to initialize the policy at the beginning of training so that the action distribution is centered around 0[10] regardless of the observation and has a rather small standard deviation. This can be achieved by initializing the policy MLP with smaller weights in the last layer (C57, Fig. 24, this alone boosts the performance on Humanoid by 66%) so that the initial action distribution is almost independent of the observation and by introducing an offset in the standard deviation of actions (C61). Fig. 2 shows that the performance is very sensitive to the initial action standard deviation with 0.5 performing best on all environments except Hopper where higher values perform better.

Figure 2: Comparison of different initial standard deviations of actions (C61).

Fig. 17 compares two approaches to transform unbounded sampled actions into the bounded [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1] domain expected by the environment (C63): clipping and applying a tanh function. tanh performs slightly better overall (in particular it improves the performance on HalfCheetah by 30% ). Fig. 17 and Fig. 2 suggests that the difference might be mostly caused by the decreased magnitude of initial actions[11] .

Other choices appear to be less important: The scale of the last layer initialization matters much less for the value MLP (C58) than for the policy MLP (Fig. 19). Apart from the last layer scaling, the network initialization scheme (C56) does not matter too much (Fig. 27). Only he_normal and he_uniform [7] appear to be suboptimal choices with the other options performing very similarly. There also appears to be no clear benefits if the standard deviation of the policy is learned for each state (i.e. outputted by the policy network) or once globally for all states (C59, Fig. 23). For the transformation of policy output into action standard deviation (C60), softplus and exponentiation perform very similarly[12] (Fig. 25). Finally, the minimum action standard deviation (C62) seems to matter little, if it is not set too large (Fig. 30).

**Recommendation.** Initialize the last policy layer with 100 _×_ smaller weights. Use softplus to transform network output into action standard deviation and add a (negative) offset to its input to decrease the initial standard deviation of actions. Tune this offset if possible. Use tanh both as the activation function (if the networks are not too deep) and to transform the samples from the normal distribution to the bounded action space. Use a wide value MLP (no layers shared with the policy) but tune the policy width (it might need to be narrower than the value MLP).

## 3.3 NORMALIZATION AND CLIPPING (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX F)

**Study description.** We investigate the impact of different normalization techniques: observation normalization (C64), value function normalization (C66), per-minibatch advantage normalization (C67), as well as gradient (C68) and observation (C65) clipping.

**Interpretation.** Input normalization (C64) is crucial for good performance on all environments apart from Hopper (Fig. 33). Quite surprisingly, value function normalization (C66) also influences the performance very strongly — it is crucial for good performance on HalfCheetah and Humanoid, helps slightly on Hopper and Ant and significantly _hurts_ the performance on Walker2d (Fig. 37). We are not sure why the value function scale matters that much but suspect that it affects the performance by changing the speed of the value function fitting.[13] In contrast to observation and value function normalization, per-minibatch advantage normalization (C67) seems not to affect the performance too much (Fig. 35). Similarly, we have found little evidence that clipping normalized[14] observations (C65) helps (Fig. 38) but it might be worth using if there is a risk of extremely high observations due to simulator divergence. Finally, gradient clipping (C68) provides a small performance boost with the exact clipping threshold making little difference (Fig. 34).
**Recommendation.** Always use observation normalization and check if value function normalization improves performance. Gradient clipping might slightly help but is of secondary importance.

## 3.4 ADVANTAGE ESTIMATION (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX G)

**Study description.** We compare the most commonly used advantage estimators (C6): N-step [3], GAE [9] and V-trace [19] and their hyperparameters (C7, C8, C9, C10). We also experiment with applying PPO-style pessimistic clipping (C13) to the value loss (present in the original PPO implementation but not mentioned in the PPO paper [17]) and using Huber loss [1] instead of MSE for value learning (C11, C12). Moreover, we varied the number of parallel environments used (C1) as it changes the length of the experience fragments collected in each step.

**Interpretation.** GAE and V-trace appear to perform better than N-step returns (Fig. 44 and 40) which indicates that it is beneficial to combine the value estimators from multiple timesteps. We have not

found a significant performance difference between GAE and V-trace in our experiments. _λ_ = 0 _._ 9 (C8, C9) performed well regardless of whether GAE (Fig. 45) or V-trace (Fig. 49) was used on all tasks but tuning this value per environment may lead to modest performance gains. We have found that PPO-style value loss clipping (C13) hurts the performance regardless of the clipping threshold[15] (Fig. 43). Similarly, the Huber loss (C11) performed worse than MSE in all environments (Fig. 42) regardless of the value of the threshold (C12) used (Fig. 48).
**Recommendation.** Use GAE with _λ_ = 0 _._ 9 but neither Huber loss nor PPO-style value loss clipping.

## 3.5 TRAINING SETUP (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX H)

**Study description.** We investigate choices related to the data collection and minibatch handling: the number of parallel environments used (C1), the number of transitions gathered in each iteration (C2), the number of passes over the data (C3), minibatch size (C4) and how the data is split into minibatches (C5).

For the last choice, in addition to standard choices, we also consider a new small modification of the original PPO approach: The original PPO implementation splits the data in each policy iteration step into individual transitions and then randomly assigns them to minibatches (C5). This makes it impossible to compute advantages as the temporal structure is broken. Therefore, the advantages are computed once at the beginning of each policy iteration step and then used in minibatch policy and value function optimization. This results in higher diversity of data in each minibatch at the cost of using slightly stale advantage estimations. As a remedy to this problem, we propose to recompute the advantages at the beginning of each pass over the data instead of just once per iteration.

**Results.** Unsurprisingly, going over the experience multiple times appears to be crucial for good sample complexity (Fig. 54). Often, this is computationally cheap due to the simple models considered, in particular on machines with accelerators such as GPUs and TPUs. As we increase the number of parallel environments (C1), performance decreases sharply on some of the environments (Fig. 55). This is likely caused by shortened experience chunks (See Sec. B.1 for the detailed description of the data collection process) and earlier value bootstrapping. Despite that, training with more environments usually leads to faster training in wall-clock time if enough CPU cores are available. Increasing the batch size (C4) does not appear to hurt the sample complexity in the range we tested (Fig. 57) which suggests that it should be increased for faster iteration speed. On the other hand, the number of transitions gathered in each iteration (C2) influences the performance quite significantly (Fig. 52). Finally, we compare different ways to handle minibatches (See App. B.1 for the detailed description of different variants) in Fig. 53 and 58. The plots suggest that stale advantages can in fact hurt performance and that recomputing them at the beginning of each pass at least partially mitigates the problem and performs best among all variants.

**Recommendation.** Go over experience multiple times. Shuffle individual transitions before assigning them to minibatches and recompute advantages once per data pass (See App. B.1 for the details). For faster wall-clock time training use many parallel environments and increase the batch size (both might hurt the sample complexity). Tune the number of transitions in each iteration (C2) if possible.

## 3.6 TIMESTEPS HANDLING (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX I)

**Study description.** We investigate choices related to the handling of timesteps: discount factor[16] (C20), frame skip (C21), and how episode termination due to timestep limits are handled (C22). The latter relates to a technical difficulty explained in App. B.4 where one assumes for the algorithm an infinite time horizon but then trains using a finite time horizon [16].

**Interpretation.** Fig. 60 shows that the performance depends heavily on the discount factor _γ_ (C20) with _γ_ = 0 _._ 99 performing reasonably well in all environments. Skipping every other frame (C21) improves the performance on 2 out of 5 environments (Fig. 61). Proper handling of episodes abandoned due to the timestep limit seems not to affect the performance (C22, Fig. 62) which

> 15This is consistent with prior work [27].

> 16While the discount factor is sometimes treated as a part of the environment, we assume that the real goal is to maximize _undiscounted_ returns and the discount factor is a part of the algorithm which makes learning easier.

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

is probably caused by the fact that the timestep limit is quite high (1000 transitions) in all the environments we considered.

**Recommendation.** Discount factor _γ_ is one of the most important hyperparameters and should be tuned per environment (start with _γ_ = 0 _._ 99). Try frame skip if possible. There is no need to handle environments step limits in a special way for large step limits.

## 3.7 OPTIMIZERS (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX J)

**Study description.** We investigate two gradient-based optimizers commonly used in RL: (C23) – Adam [8] and RMSprop – as well as their hyperparameters (C24, C25, C26, C27, C28, C29, C30) and a linear learning rate decay schedule (C31).

> **Interpretation.** The differences in performance between the optimizers (C23) appear to be rather small with no optimizer consistently outperforming the other across environments (Fig. 66). Unsurprisingly, the learning rate influences the performance very strongly (Fig. 69) with the default value of 0 _._ 0003 for Adam (C24) performing well on all tasks. Fig. 67 shows that Adam works better with momentum (C26). For RMSprop, momentum (C27) makes less difference (Fig. 71) but our results suggest that it might slightly improve performance[17] . Whether the centered or uncentered version of RMSprop is used (C30) makes no difference (Fig. 70) and similarly we did not find any difference between different values of the _ϵ_ coefficients (C28, C29, Fig. 68 and 72). Linearly decaying the learning rate to 0 increases the performance on 4 out of 5 tasks but the gains are very small apart from Ant, where it leads to 15% higher scores (Fig. 65).

**Recommendation.** Use Adam [8] optimizer with momentum _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9 and a tuned learning rate (0 _._ 0003 is a safe default). Linearly decaying the learning rate may slightly improve performance but is of secondary importance.

## 3.8 REGULARIZATION (BASED ON THE RESULTS IN APPENDIX K)

**Study description.** We investigate different policy regularizers (C32), which can have either the form of a penalty (C33, e.g. bonus for higher entropy) or a soft constraint (C34, e.g. entropy should not be lower than some threshold) which is enforced with a Lagrange multiplier. In particular, we consider the following regularization terms: entropy (C40, C46), the Kullback–Leibler divergence (KL) between a reference _N_ (0 _,_ 1) action distribution and the current policy (C37, C43) and the KL divergence and reverse KL divergence between the current policy and the behavioral one (C35, C41, C36, C42), as well as the “decoupled” KL divergence from [18, 34] (C38, C39, C44, C45).

**Interpretation.** We do not find evidence that regularization helps significantly on our environments with the exception of HalfCheetah on which all constraints (especially the entropy constraint) help (Fig. 76 and 77). However, the performance boost is largely independent on the constraint threshold (Fig. 83, 84, 87, 89, 90 and 91) which suggests that the effect is caused by the initial high strength of the penalty (before it gets adjusted) and not by the desired constraint. While it is surprising that regularization does not help at all (apart from HalfCheetah), we conjecture that regularization might be less important in our experiments because: (1) the PPO policy loss already enforces the trust region which makes KL penalties or constraints redundant; and (2) the careful policy initialization (See Sec. 3.2) is enough to guarantee good exploration making the entropy bonus or constraint redundant.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Islam et al. [15] and Henderson et al. [22] point out reproducibility issues in RL including the performance differences between different code bases, the importance of hyperparameter tuning and the high level of stochasticity due to random seeds. Tucker et al. [26] showed that the gains, which had been attributed to one of the recently proposed policy gradients improvements, were, in fact, caused by the implementation details. The most closely related work to ours is probably Engstrom et al. [27] where the authors investigate code-level improvements in the PPO [17] code base and conclude that they are responsible for the most of the performance difference between PPO

and TRPO [10]. Our work is also similar to other large-scale studies done in other fields of Deep Learning, e.g. model-based RL [31], GANs [24], NLP [35], disentangled representations [23] and convolution network architectures [36].

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we investigated the importance of a broad set of high- and low-level choices that need to be made when designing and implementing on-policy deep actor-critic RL algorithms. Based on more than 250’000 experiments in five continuous control environments, we evaluate the impact of different choices and provide practical recommendations. One of the surprising insights is that the initial action distribution plays an important role in agent performance. We expect this to be a fruitful avenue for future research.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a large-scale empirical study of over 50 implementation choices in on-policy deep actor-critic reinforcement learning, training over 250,000 agents across five continuous control environments. It provides practical insights into the sensitivity of these algorithms to low-level design decisions, such as network initialization and normalization strategies.

The paper provides a valuable, high-effort empirical contribution that addresses the 'reproducibility crisis' in deep reinforcement learning by systematically isolating implementation details. Its findings on the importance of initial policy distribution and value function normalization offer actionable guidance to practitioners in the field.

Overview issues:

- Confounding variables in hyperparameter interactions: Section 2.6 acknowledges that many choices interact, yet the experimental design relies on grouping choices and sampling them randomly within those groups. While the authors include the Adam learning rate (C24) in all groups, this does not fully account for higher-order interactions between, for example, batch size (C4) and gradient clipping (C68) or network architecture (C47). By reporting the 95th percentile of performance across random samples, the paper risks masking optimal configurations that only emerge from specific, non-random combinations of hyperparameters. The study would be more convincing if it included a sensitivity analysis demonstrating that the reported 'best' choices remain optimal even when interacting parameters are perturbed.

- Limited generalizability to diverse action spaces: The methodology is restricted to continuous control environments (Hopper, Walker2d, HalfCheetah, Ant, Humanoid) from OpenAI Gym. The paper makes broad recommendations for 'on-policy deep actor-critic' methods, but these conclusions may not hold for discrete action spaces or high-dimensional image-based inputs often used in Atari-style tasks. The authors should explicitly qualify their recommendations as specific to continuous control or include at least one discrete-action environment to demonstrate that their findings regarding initialization and normalization are not artifacts of the continuous action domain.

- Unclear impact of reward scaling: The paper identifies that value function normalization (C66) is crucial for performance (Section 3.3), yet it does not systematically investigate the interaction between reward scales and the effectiveness of this normalization. Different environments have vastly different reward magnitudes, which can significantly alter the gradient dynamics of the critic. A more rigorous approach would involve testing the sensitivity of the proposed 'best' settings against varying reward scales to determine if the observed performance gains are robust or if they are merely compensating for poor reward scaling in the specific environments tested.

- Independence of implementation choices: The study assumes that the performance impact of design choices is largely additive, allowing for the evaluation of choices in thematic groups. However, deep RL training is notoriously non-linear, where a single poor choice (e.g., incorrect weight initialization) can lead to complete training failure regardless of other settings. The paper should provide more evidence that the 'base configuration' used for each group is not biased toward specific outcomes, or perform a subset of experiments to verify that the optimal choices identified in isolation perform well when combined into a single 'best' agent.

- Validation of the 'Best' Combined Configuration: The paper identifies optimal settings for individual design choices through ablation studies, but it never demonstrates that combining these optimal choices results in a superior agent compared to the baseline. Given the non-linear nature of deep reinforcement learning, there is a risk that these individually optimal choices might conflict or lead to unstable training when aggregated. The authors should explicitly test a 'best-of-all' configuration—using the recommended settings from each section—and compare its performance against the baseline configuration used in the study to verify the additive value of their recommendations.

- Lack of Sensitivity Analysis for Reward Scaling: The authors identify value function normalization as crucial, yet they do not investigate how this choice interacts with varying reward scales. Because different environments have significantly different reward magnitudes, the effectiveness of normalization techniques likely depends on the underlying reward distribution. To make the recommendation for value function normalization robust, the paper should include a controlled experiment where the reward scale is artificially varied (e.g., by multiplying rewards by a factor of 0.1, 1, and 10) to determine if the recommended normalization strategy remains effective across these regimes.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Add a section or appendix explicitly discussing the limitations of the additive assumption in the experimental design.

- Include a verification experiment where the 'recommended' settings are combined and compared against the baseline to confirm the cumulative performance gain.

- Clarify in the abstract and conclusion that the recommendations are specifically targeted at continuous control tasks.

comments.title: Statistical interpretation of the 95th percentile
comments.quote: Intuitively, this corresponds to a robust estimate of the performance one can expect if all other choices in the group were tuned with random search and a limited budget of roughly 20 hyperparameter configurations.
comments.explanation: The claim that the 95th percentile estimates performance for a random search budget of 20 configurations is statistically unsupported. With 20 samples, the probability of selecting at least one configuration from the top 5% of the population is approximately 0.64, which does not guarantee that the best sample will reach the 95th percentile. Please clarify the statistical justification for this equivalence or remove the specific budget claim.

comments.title: Contradiction in batch size impact
comments.quote: Increasing the batch size (C4) does not appear to hurt the sample complexity in the range we tested (Fig. 57) which suggests that it should be increased for faster iteration speed. On the other hand, the number of transitions gathered in each iteration (C2) influences the performance quite significantly (Fig. 52).
comments.explanation: The text claims increasing batch size (C4) does not hurt sample complexity, yet the recommendation section later warns that it 'might hurt the sample complexity.' Furthermore, C4 is typically a subset of the data collected in an iteration (C2). Please clarify the relationship between these parameters and reconcile the contradictory statements regarding their impact on sample efficiency.

comments.title: Ambiguity in value function normalization impact
comments.quote: Quite surprisingly, value function normalization (C66) also influences the performance very strongly — it is crucial for good performance on HalfCheetah and Humanoid, helps slightly on Hopper and Ant and significantly hurts the performance on Walker2d (Fig. 37).
comments.explanation: Describing value function normalization as 'crucial' while simultaneously noting it significantly hurts performance on Walker2d is contradictory. This suggests the effect is highly environment-dependent rather than a universal requirement. Please revise this to acknowledge that the benefit is conditional on environment dynamics rather than a general recommendation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in regularization conclusions
comments.quote: However, the performance boost is largely independent on the constraint threshold (Fig. 83, 84, 87, 89, 90 and 91) which suggests that the effect is caused by the initial high strength of the penalty (before it gets adjusted) and not by the desired constraint.
comments.explanation: The authors conclude that regularization is 'less important' because the threshold-based constraint is redundant, yet they simultaneously show that the initial penalty strength is a significant factor in performance. If the initial penalty is a necessary condition for the observed gains, the mechanism is functionally critical. Please rephrase the conclusion to distinguish between the efficacy of the constraint versus the impact of the initial penalty.

comments.title: Overstatement of experimental scope
comments.quote: Based on more than 250’000 experiments in five continuous control environments, we evaluate the impact of different choices and provide practical recommendations.
comments.explanation: The claim that the study provides 'practical recommendations' for 'on-policy deep actor-critic RL algorithms' is too broad, as the experiments are restricted to continuous control. These findings may not generalize to discrete action spaces or image-based inputs. Please qualify these recommendations as specific to continuous control tasks to avoid misleading readers.

comments.title: Ambiguity in performance metric definition
comments.quote: We evaluate trained policies every hundred thousand steps by freezing the policy and computing the average undiscounted episode return of 100 episodes (with the stochastic policy). We then average these score to obtain a single performance score of the seed which is proportional to the area under the learning curve.
comments.explanation: The claim that the performance score is 'proportional to the area under the learning curve' is imprecise because the number of evaluation points varies between environments. A simple mean of returns at checkpoints does not necessarily represent the integral of the learning curve. Please redefine the performance score as the arithmetic mean of returns at evaluation checkpoints, serving as a proxy for average performance.

comments.title: Inconsistency in learning rate decay recommendation
comments.quote: early decaying the learning rate to $0$ increases the performance on $4$ out of $5$ tasks but the gains are very small apart from Ant, where it leads to $15\%$ higher scores (Fig.
comments.explanation: The recommendation dismisses linear learning rate decay as 'secondary importance' despite reporting a 15% performance gain on the Ant environment. In deep RL, a 15% improvement is significant. Please reconcile this by acknowledging that while the effect is small on average, it is non-negligible for specific tasks.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
This paper conducts a large-scale empirical study to identify which low- and high-level design choices most significantly impact the performance of on-policy deep actor-critic methods. The authors implement over 50 configurable choices within a unified framework, train more than 250,000 agents across five continuous control environments, and analyze the results using conditional 95th percentile performance and top-5% distribution metrics. The work addresses a genuine reproducibility gap in reinforcement learning by systematically isolating implementation details that are often glossed over in algorithmic papers.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by providing a highly configurable implementation and a massive empirical dataset that isolates the impact of common RL engineering choices. The systematic grouping of choices and the focus on practical recommendations offer actionable guidance for practitioners building on-policy agents. The sheer scale of the experiments and the transparent reporting of raw data and scripts are significant strengths that elevate the work beyond typical ablation studies.

Overview issues:

- Limited and Outdated Environment Scope: The study evaluates all findings exclusively on five continuous control tasks from OpenAI Gym v1. These environments rely on legacy MuJoCo 1.50 backends and lack the physical fidelity and reward structures of modern benchmarks like Brax or MuJoCo 2.0. Because dynamics and reward scaling heavily influence hyperparameter sensitivity, recommendations derived from Gym v1 may not transfer to current standard benchmarks. The paper does not acknowledge this limitation or test whether the observed sensitivities persist in newer simulators. I recommend either restricting the paper’s claims to legacy Gym v1 environments or adding a small validation set using modern MuJoCo/Brax tasks to demonstrate generalizability.

- Uniform Hyperparameter Sampling Undermines Fair Comparisons: Section 2.7 states that choices are sampled randomly within thematic groups, but the text does not specify whether the sampling is uniform or log-uniform. Uniform sampling over learning rates, batch sizes, and GAE λ values is notoriously inefficient for reinforcement learning and systematically biases results toward poorly scaled regions. This approach makes it difficult to fairly compare policy losses that have different hyperparameter sensitivities, as some losses may simply fall into dead zones more often. The authors should explicitly define the sampling distributions and justify them. Switching to log-uniform sampling for scale-dependent parameters or employing a structured design like Latin Hypercube would yield more reliable conclusions about which choices truly matter.

- Insufficient Statistical Rigor for High-Variance Comparisons: The performance metric relies on the median of only three random seeds per configuration, which is insufficient to capture the high variance inherent in actor-critic training. While the conditional 95th percentile analysis mitigates some noise, the main comparative plots in Sections 3.1 through 3.8 lack confidence intervals or formal statistical tests. Readers cannot determine whether the reported advantages of PPO over V-trace or tanh over ReLU are statistically significant or driven by seed-specific luck. I suggest increasing the seed count to at least ten for the final configuration comparisons and reporting standard errors. Adding bootstrap-based significance testing for the top-performing choices would strengthen the empirical claims.

- Contradictory Framing of Regularization Findings: Section 3.8 concludes that entropy and KL regularization are largely redundant for on-policy deep actor-critic methods, with the exception of HalfCheetah. This finding directly conflicts with the widespread use of entropy bonuses in off-policy algorithms like SAC and the KL constraints in TRPO. The paper acknowledges that PPO’s clipping acts as a trust region but does not explain why entropy maximization fails to provide additional exploration benefits in this specific setting. Without a theoretical or empirical bridge, the recommendation to drop regularization may mislead practitioners working in off-policy or model-based contexts. The authors should explicitly limit this conclusion to clipped on-policy policy gradients and discuss how regularization interacts with the specific advantage estimators and loss functions tested.

- Non-Standard Performance Metric Obscures Asymptotic Results: Section 2.8 defines the performance score as the average of undiscounted episode returns evaluated every 100,000 steps, then averaged across evaluation points. This area-under-the-curve approach conflates early learning speed with final asymptotic performance, making it difficult to compare against standard RL reporting conventions. Many practitioners care primarily about the final return after a fixed budget, not how quickly the agent reaches it. The metric also complicates direct comparison with prior large-scale studies that use final return or mean over the last N evaluations. I recommend supplementing this metric with standard final-return reporting or clearly justifying why the averaged evaluation score is preferred. If retained, the metric should be explicitly mapped to conventional benchmarks in the analysis.

- Missing Baseline Validation for the Unified Implementation: The authors verify that the unified implementation matches OpenAI baselines PPO but do not benchmark against other independent, widely used PPO implementations such as CleanRL or RLlib. Without cross-implementation validation, it is unclear whether the observed sensitivities stem from the underlying algorithm or from hidden biases in the SEED RL codebase. The unified framework itself becomes a confounding variable when interpreting why certain choices matter. I suggest adding a baseline comparison against at least two independent PPO implementations to confirm that the framework reproduces established performance ceilings. This would isolate the ablation study’s findings from implementation-specific artifacts.

- Lack of theoretical grounding for policy initialization findings: The paper empirically demonstrates that initializing the policy with a zero-mean, low-standard deviation, and observation-independent action distribution significantly improves training speed. However, it offers no theoretical or worked example to explain why this initialization works. Readers will wonder why this specific initialization prevents early policy collapse or gradient issues. The authors should compute the expected gradient or policy divergence for a simple parametric model, such as a linear Gaussian policy on a linear-quadratic task, to show how poor initialization leads to high initial KL divergence or vanishing gradients. Providing this worked special case would ground the empirical finding and help practitioners understand the mechanism behind the recommendation.

- Missing verification that implemented losses recover standard formulations: The study evaluates multiple policy losses but never formally verifies that the unified implementation recovers the original algorithmic formulations as special cases. While the authors state they match OpenAI baselines PPO, they do not show that the loss equations mathematically reduce to the standard definitions when hyperparameters are set to their canonical values. This is a standard expectation for a paper claiming to implement and compare algorithmic variants. The authors should include a brief derivation or table mapping each implemented loss to its original mathematical formulation, confirming that the unified code correctly implements the theoretical objectives before drawing performance conclusions.

- Insufficient exploration of boundary conditions for regularization claims: The paper concludes that entropy and KL regularization are largely redundant for on-policy deep actor-critic methods, attributing this to PPO's clipping and careful initialization. This claim directly conflicts with the widespread use of entropy bonuses in off-policy algorithms and the KL constraints in trust-region methods. The authors do not develop the implications of this finding or discuss the boundary conditions under which regularization might become necessary. For instance, the paper does not test whether regularization helps in sparse reward settings, or with different advantage estimators that might produce higher variance gradients. Adding a small ablation on a sparse-reward variant of the tested environments or discussing how the findings scale to off-policy contexts would prevent practitioners from misapplying these recommendations.

- Lack of discussion on computational trade-offs for recommended choices: The paper provides practical recommendations such as recomputing advantages at the beginning of each data pass and using many parallel environments. However, it does not discuss the computational and memory overheads of these choices in distributed training settings. Recomputing advantages multiple times increases gradient computation costs, and scaling parallel environments requires careful synchronization. The authors should acknowledge these trade-offs and provide guidance on how to balance sample complexity against wall-clock time and memory constraints. A brief discussion on the scalability of the recommended hyperparameter configurations to large-scale distributed setups would make the practical recommendations more actionable for practitioners.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace uniform hyperparameter sampling with log-uniform distributions or structured designs (e.g., Latin Hypercube) for all scale-dependent parameters and explicitly document the sampling strategy.

- Increase the seed count to at least ten for final configuration comparisons, report standard errors on all main comparative plots, and add bootstrap or non-parametric significance testing for key claims.

- Benchmark the unified implementation against at least two independent, widely maintained PPO codebases to rule out framework-specific biases before presenting ablation results.

- Clarify the scope of the regularization findings, explicitly limit recommendations to clipped on-policy policy gradients, and discuss why entropy/KL constraints behave differently in this setting compared to off-policy or maximum-entropy frameworks.

- Add standard final-return reporting alongside the current evaluation protocol, or provide a clear justification for the averaged evaluation metric and map it to conventional benchmarks used in prior large-scale RL studies.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of standard PPO advantage computation
comments.quote: The original PPO implementation splits the data in each policy iteration step into individual transitions and then randomly assigns them to minibatches (C5). This makes it impossible to compute advantages as the temporal structure is broken.
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents the standard PPO procedure. Advantages are computed on the complete buffer of collected trajectories before any minibatch sampling occurs. The advantage vector is calculated once per iteration using GAE, and only then is the dataset shuffled and partitioned. The temporal structure required for advantage calculation is preserved because computation happens on the full rollout buffer. Correcting this description is necessary to validate the motivation for your proposed modification.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of trust-region properties to RPA
comments.quote: Overall, these results show that trust-region optimization (preventing the current policy from diverging too much from the behavioral one) which is present in all the other policy losses is crucial for good sample complexity.
comments.explanation: RPA is defined as the limiting case of AWR where beta approaches zero, reducing to a simple regression on actions with positive advantages. It lacks any trust-region mechanism or constraint to prevent policy divergence. Attributing a trust-region property to RPA is mathematically incorrect. Revise the statement to specify that trust-region properties apply only to PPO, AWR, and V-MPO, or reframe the conclusion around the shared use of policy constraints rather than a unified trust-region framework.

comments.title: PPO clipping does not strictly enforce a KL trust region
comments.quote: the PPO policy loss already enforces the trust region which makes KL penalties or constraints redundant
comments.explanation: PPO-clip bounds the importance sampling ratio, which only approximates a KL constraint and does not strictly enforce one. A bounded ratio does not guarantee a bounded KL divergence, especially in high-dimensional action spaces where probability mass can shift significantly while keeping ratios within bounds. Claiming strict enforcement is inaccurate. State that clipping approximates a trust region and may reduce the marginal benefit of explicit KL penalties instead.

comments.title: Initialization cannot guarantee exploration throughout training
comments.quote: the careful policy initialization (See Sec. 3.2) is enough to guarantee good exploration making the entropy bonus or constraint redundant.
comments.explanation: Initialization only sets the starting policy parameters. It does not control how the policy explores the environment over the course of training. Exploration depends on the policy's stochasticity, the environment's dynamics, and the optimization trajectory. Early in training, policies can still collapse to deterministic behaviors despite careful initialization. Attribute the benefit to the starting distribution rather than implying a guarantee of ongoing exploration.

comments.title: Statistical intuition for 95th percentile is methodologically ambiguous
comments.quote: Intuitively, this corresponds to a robust estimate of the performance one can expect if all other choices in the group were tuned with random search and a limited budget of roughly 20 hyperparameter configurations.
comments.explanation: The intuition conflates the population 95th percentile with the expected best performance from a fixed sample budget. Estimating a tail quantile from roughly 20 samples is statistically noisy and contradicts the claim of a robust estimate. The text does not clarify whether the actual number of configurations per choice value is much larger. Clarify the actual sample size per condition and remove the misleading intuition, or explicitly derive the relationship between the reported percentile and the stated budget.

comments.title: Inconsistent scaling of performance score across environments
comments.quote: We then average these score to obtain a single performance score of the seed which is proportional to the area under the learning curve.
comments.explanation: The number of evaluation points differs across environments: 10 points for the 1M-step tasks and 20 points for the 2M-step tasks. Averaging the returns over a different number of points changes the metric's scale by a factor of two between these groups. The reported performance scores are not directly comparable across environments. Normalize the average by the number of evaluation points to maintain a consistent scale, or explicitly restrict comparisons to within each environment.

comments.title: Recommendation contradicts strong negative finding on Walker2d
comments.quote: Recommendation. Always use observation normalization and check if value function normalization improves performance. Gradient clipping might slightly help but is of secondary importance.
comments.explanation: The interpretation explicitly states that value function normalization significantly hurts the performance on Walker2d, yet the recommendation advises readers to check if it improves performance. This phrasing is overly optimistic and ignores the strong negative result on a standard benchmark. Revise the recommendation to reflect the task-dependent nature of this finding, such as advising caution or explicitly stating that its benefit is not universal.

comments.title: Misattributed cause for GAE/V-trace superiority over N-step
comments.quote: GAE and V-trace appear to perform better than N-step returns (Fig. 44 and 40) which indicates that it is beneficial to combine the value estimators from multiple timesteps.
comments.explanation: The comparison does not isolate the factor of combining value estimators, because N-step returns inherently do exactly that by bootstrapping over n steps. The performance difference likely stems from how GAE manages the bias-variance tradeoff via the lambda parameter or how V-trace handles variance through importance sampling. State that GAE and V-trace outperform N-step returns because they dynamically adjust the bias-variance tradeoff or reduce estimator variance, rather than simply combining estimators.

comments.title: Tension between OFAT warning and actual methodology
comments.quote: experiments where only a single choice is varied but interacting choices are kept fixed may lead to misleading conclusions.
comments.explanation: This caution directly conflicts with the structure of Sections 3.1 through 3.8, where the entire analysis relies on isolating single choices while holding others constant. Readers will immediately notice this discrepancy between the stated principle and the executed design. Address this compromise explicitly rather than leaving it unexplained. Acknowledge that one-factor-at-a-time variations are used here to isolate individual effects before testing broader combinations.

comments.title: Unmotivated choices introduce confounding variables
comments.quote: Whenever we were faced with implementation decisions that required us to take decisions that could not be clearly motivated or had alternative solutions, we further added such decisions as additional choices.
comments.explanation: This approach introduces uncontrolled nuisance parameters into the ablation study. Adding configuration options specifically for decisions that could not be clearly motivated means these choices lack a theoretical or empirical basis, making it difficult to isolate their effects from other hyperparameters. Restrict added choices to those with clear motivation, or explicitly describe how you will account for interactions with these unexplained choices during the analysis.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of V-trace design purpose
comments.quote: the V-trace loss is targeted at near-on-policy data in a distributed setting
comments.explanation: V-trace was explicitly designed to handle off-policy data, not near-on-policy data. The algorithm introduces importance sampling ratios and value bootstrapping to correct for distributional shift in distributed, off-policy settings. Describing it as targeted at near-on-policy data contradicts its foundational motivation. Correct this clause to state that V-trace is targeted at off-policy data in distributed settings.

comments.title: Conflation of training speed and asymptotic performance
comments.quote: In particular, we have found that initializing the network so that the initial action distribution has zero mean, a rather low standard deviation and is independent of the observation significantly improves the training speed (Sec. 3.2).
comments.explanation: Training speed and asymptotic performance are distinct metrics. The paper’s primary evaluation metric is the average undiscounted episode return, not convergence rate. Readers may incorrectly infer that this initialization improves final task performance rather than just how quickly the agent learns. Clarify whether the initialization affects asymptotic performance, sample efficiency, or both, and align the claim with the specific metric reported in Section 3.2.

comments.title: Vague definition of on-policy learning
comments.quote: We define on-policy learning in the following loose sense: We consider policy iteration algorithms that iterate between generating experience using the current policy and using that experience to improve the policy.
comments.explanation: This phrasing describes general policy iteration rather than the specific constraint that distinguishes on-policy methods. On-policy learning strictly requires that the data collection distribution matches the policy being updated at the time of collection. The very next sentence admits that multiple updates generate off-policy data, which creates a logical tension with the stated definition. Clarify the boundary between the data collection phase and the update phase.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about exponential failure probability
comments.quote: If there are many choices, the probability of such failure increases exponentially.
comments.explanation: This sentence presents a heuristic observation as a strict mathematical law without derivation or citation. The probability of sampling a poor configuration depends on the volume of the well-performing region in the hyperparameter space, which does not universally shrink exponentially with dimensionality. Replace the vague assertion with a precise geometric explanation or a reference to standard random search literature.

comments.title: Logical tension regarding initialization importance
comments.quote: Apart from the last layer scaling, the network initialization scheme (C56) does not matter too much (Fig. 27). Only he_normal and he_uniform [7] appear to be suboptimal choices with the other options performing very similarly.
comments.explanation: The statement that initialization does not matter too much is immediately followed by singling out He initialization as suboptimal, which creates a slight logical tension. Since He initialization is specifically designed for ReLU networks and the authors explicitly recommend tanh activations, the suboptimality of He is mathematically expected. Add a brief explanation linking the suboptimality to the mismatch between He's ReLU-specific variance scaling and the recommended tanh activations.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of timestep limits
comments.quote:
. 61). Proper handling of episodes abandoned due to the timestep limit seems not to affect the performance (C22, Fig. 62) which

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

is probably caused by the fact that the timestep limit is quite high ($1000$ transitions) in all the environments we con
comments.explanation: The explanation attributes the lack of performance difference to the timestep limit being quite high, but 1000 steps is the standard default limit in OpenAI Gym v1 MuJoCo environments, not an unusually high value. The negligible effect is more accurately explained by the fact that agents rarely reach the truncation boundary during training. Rephrase to reflect that the limit is the standard default and that the lack of impact stems from agents rarely hitting the boundary.

comments.title: Imprecise terminology regarding Adam's momentum
comments.quote: ig. 66). Unsurprisingly, the learning rate influences the performance very strongly (Fig. 69) with the default value of $0.0003$ for Adam (C24) performing well on all tasks. Fig. 67 shows that Adam works better with momentum
comments.explanation: Adam inherently includes momentum via the first moment estimate, so describing it as working better with momentum implies the component is optional. This contradicts the standard algorithm definition. The authors likely compared different values of beta_1 or tested a variant without the first moment update. Clarify that the standard Adam optimizer always includes momentum and specify which hyperparameter was actually varied.

comments.title: Ambiguous reporting of percentage score improvements
comments.quote: early decaying the learning rate to $0$ increases the performance on $4$ out of $5$ tasks but the gains are very small apart from Ant, where it leads to $15\%$ higher scores (Fig.
comments.explanation: Reporting performance gains as a percentage is ambiguous in reinforcement learning, where returns can be negative or vary widely in scale across environments. A 15% change could refer to relative improvement or absolute point difference, and percentage metrics become misleading when baseline scores are near zero or negative. Add the absolute score difference or explicitly state whether the percentage is relative to the baseline return.

comments.title: Misattribution of reproducibility findings to Henderson et al.
comments.quote: m et al. *[15]* and Henderson et al. *[22]* point out reproducibility issues in RL including the performance differences between different code bases, the
comments.explanation: Henderson et al. (2018) primarily focuses on systematically evaluating deep reinforcement learning algorithms across a wide range of environments to assess their performance and robustness, rather than investigating reproducibility issues across different codebases. The claim about performance differences stemming from codebase variations is central to Islam et al. (2017) and later Engstrom et al. (2020), but not to Henderson et al. (2018). Separate these citations to accurately reflect each paper's specific focus.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Engstrom et al. findings
comments.quote:
he most closely related work to ours is probably Engstrom et al. *[27]* where the authors investigate code-level improvements in the PPO *[17]* code base and conclude that they are responsible for the most of the performance difference between PPO

<!-- PAGE B
comments.explanation: The description of Engstrom et al. (2020) is inaccurate. The paper conducted a systematic comparison of PPO and TRPO across multiple implementation details, including optimizers, gradient clipping, and network architectures. The authors concluded that these implementation choices and hyperparameter settings, rather than the algorithmic differences between PPO and TRPO, account for the observed performance gap. Frame the result as solely due to PPO code improvements misrepresents the paper's core contribution. Revise this sentence to accurately reflect the comparative nature of the study.

comments.title: Clarify impact of initialization on speed vs. performance
comments.quote: One of the surprising insights is that the initial action distribution plays an important role in agent performance.
comments.explanation: The empirical results in Section 3.2 and the accompanying analysis explicitly attribute the benefits of careful policy initialization to faster convergence and the prevention of early policy collapse, rather than improvements in asymptotic returns. By stating that the initial action distribution affects agent performance, the conclusion risks conflating sample efficiency with final task mastery. This distinction matters because practitioners evaluating the paper's recommendations need to know whether to expect quicker training or higher final scores.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This large-scale empirical study investigates over 50 design choices in on-policy deep actor-critic methods for continuous control. The paper is well-motivated and provides practical recommendations based on extensive experimentation. However, several methodological limitations and framing issues reduce the strength of its conclusions.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by systematically ablating many implementation choices in a unified framework, confirming some known practices and revealing surprising findings (e.g., policy initialization). Its main strength is the sheer scale of experiments (250,000+ agents) and the clear, actionable recommendations. However, the study's conclusions are limited by the narrow set of environments (only MuJoCo continuous control tasks), the lack of statistical rigor (only 3 seeds per configuration, no correction for multiple comparisons), and insufficient discussion of interactions between choices. The paper would be stronger with broader environmental coverage, more seeds, and a more nuanced treatment of interactions.

Overview issues:

- Limited environmental diversity restricts generalizability: The study uses only five MuJoCo continuous control environments (Hopper, Walker2d, HalfCheetah, Ant, Humanoid). These are all locomotion tasks with similar state/action spaces and reward structures. The paper's recommendations (e.g., "use PPO policy loss", "use GAE with λ=0.9") may not transfer to other domains like robotics manipulation, game playing, or discrete action spaces. The authors acknowledge this limitation briefly in the introduction but do not discuss how their findings might change in other settings. A more diverse set of environments (including at least one discrete-action or visual-input task) would significantly strengthen the generalizability claims.

- Insufficient statistical rigor in experimental design: The paper uses only 3 random seeds per configuration and reports median performance. While the authors argue that their conditional 95th percentile analysis aggregates over hundreds of configurations, this does not replace proper statistical testing (e.g., confidence intervals on mean performance, hypothesis tests for differences between choices). The confidence intervals reported for the 95th percentile are based on a binomial approximation, which may be inaccurate for small sample sizes. Furthermore, no correction for multiple comparisons is applied when evaluating over 50 choices across 5 environments. This increases the risk of false positive findings. The paper would benefit from reporting standard errors, using more seeds (at least 5-10), and applying appropriate multiple-testing corrections.

- Confounding interactions between choices are not adequately addressed: The paper groups choices into thematic categories and varies only one group at a time while keeping others fixed at a base configuration. This design assumes that interactions between groups are negligible, which is unlikely. For example, the optimal learning rate (C24) may depend on the policy loss (C14) or network architecture (C47-62). The authors acknowledge this limitation in Section 2 but do not conduct any experiments to quantify interaction effects. Without such analysis, recommendations like "use Adam with learning rate 0.0003" may be valid only for the specific base configuration tested. A factorial design or interaction analysis (e.g., ANOVA) would help identify which choices truly have independent effects.

- Lack of comparison with off-policy or hybrid methods limits practical relevance: While the paper focuses on on-policy methods, many practitioners choose between on-policy and off-policy algorithms for continuous control. The paper does not compare its tuned on-policy configuration against strong off-policy baselines like SAC or TD3. This omission makes it difficult to assess whether the recommended on-policy setup is competitive in absolute terms. Including such comparisons (even as a secondary analysis) would help readers decide when to use on-policy methods versus alternatives.

- Incomplete documentation of hyperparameter tuning and base configuration: The paper relies on a "competitive base configuration" (Appendix C) that is close to PPOv2 scaled to 256 parallel environments. However, the process for selecting this base configuration is not described—was it tuned on a held-out set? How sensitive are the results to this choice? The paper also does not report the range of hyperparameters sampled for each choice (e.g., learning rate min/max, number of layers). Without this information, it is impossible to assess whether the observed effects are due to the choice itself or the specific range tested. The authors should provide full hyperparameter ranges and justify their selection.

- Performance metric conflates learning speed and final performance: The paper uses a performance score proportional to the area under the learning curve, which equally weights early and late performance. This metric favors algorithms that learn quickly, but in practice, final asymptotic performance is often more important. The paper does not report final performance separately, so it is unclear whether the recommendations improve sample efficiency or ultimate solution quality. Reporting both metrics (e.g., final return and AUC) would provide a more complete picture.

- No analysis of computational cost or wall-clock time: The paper mentions that using more parallel environments increases wall-clock speed but hurts sample complexity. However, it provides no quantitative analysis of computational cost (e.g., GPU hours, total training time). For practitioners, the trade-off between sample efficiency and wall-clock time is critical. The paper should include a systematic comparison of computational requirements for different choices (e.g., number of environments, batch size) to help readers make informed decisions.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper addresses an important question and provides a useful empirical resource, but several methodological weaknesses limit the reliability and generalizability of its conclusions. The revision should address the limited environment diversity, improve statistical rigor, and provide more thorough analysis of interactions and computational costs.

Revision targets:

- Add at least 2-3 additional environments from different domains (e.g., discrete action, visual input, or robotics manipulation) to test generalizability of recommendations.

- Increase number of seeds per configuration to at least 5-10 and report standard errors or confidence intervals on mean performance. Apply multiple-testing corrections where appropriate.

- Conduct a small factorial experiment to quantify interactions between at least two groups of choices (e.g., policy loss × learning rate, or network architecture × normalization).

- Include a comparison against strong off-policy baselines (SAC, TD3) using the recommended on-policy configuration to contextualize absolute performance.

- Report final asymptotic performance separately from area-under-curve, and include a computational cost analysis (e.g., wall-clock time, GPU hours) for key choices.

comments.title: Confidence interval method is misapplied
comments.quote: We report the 95th percentile of the performance as well as a confidence interval based on a binomial approximation . Intuitively, this corresponds to a robust estimate of the performance one can expect if all other choices in the group were tuned with random search and a limited budget of roughly 20 hyperparameter configurations.
comments.explanation: The binomial approximation for a confidence interval on a percentile is only valid when the sample size is large enough that the number of observations below the percentile is approximately normal. With only 20 configurations per value (as stated), the approximation is unreliable — the 95th percentile is the maximum of the sample, and its sampling distribution is highly skewed. The authors should either use a nonparametric bootstrap to estimate the confidence interval or report the exact binomial interval (e.g., using the beta distribution). The current approach likely produces intervals that are too narrow, giving a false sense of precision.

comments.title: Top-5% analysis conflates importance with noise
comments.quote: By design of the experiment, values for each choice are distributed uniformly at random. Thus, if certain values are over-represented in the top models, this indicates that the specific choice is important in guaranteeing good performance.
comments.explanation: This reasoning is flawed. Even if a choice has no effect on performance, random variation will cause some values to be over-represented in the top 5% by chance. The paper does not provide a statistical test (e.g., chi-squared or permutation test) to distinguish signal from noise. Without such a test, the analysis may identify spurious "important" choices. The authors should either report a p-value or effect size for the over-representation, or use a more robust method like a permutation test that compares the observed distribution to what would be expected under the null hypothesis of no effect.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of on-policy learning
comments.quote: We define on-policy learning in the following loose sense: We consider policy iteration algorithms that iterate between generating experience using the current policy and using that experience to improve the policy.
