This is the standard modus operandi of algorithms usually considered on-policy such as PPO *[17]*. However, we note that algorithms often perform several model updates and thus may operate technically on off-policy data within a single policy improvement iteration.
comments.explanation: The definition of on-policy learning is internally contradictory. The first sentence defines it as generating experience with the current policy and using that experience to improve it, which is the standard definition. But the third sentence says algorithms 'often perform several model updates and thus may operate technically on off-policy data within a single policy improvement iteration.' This creates confusion: if multiple updates are allowed, then the data used for later updates is off-policy relative to the behavior policy that generated it. The paper should either adopt a stricter definition (e.g., one update per iteration) or explicitly state that it allows multiple updates and acknowledge that this makes the setting technically off-policy. The current phrasing undermines the paper's claim of studying on-policy methods.

comments.title: Verification claim lacks quantitative evidence
comments.quote: We verified that when all choices are selected as in the PPO implementation from OpenAI baselines, we obtain similar performance as reported in the PPO paper *[17]*.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to have verified that their unified implementation reproduces the PPO paper's results, but no quantitative evidence is provided in this section or referenced concretely. The phrase 'similar performance' is vague — it is unclear whether this means the same mean return, same variance, or same learning curves. Without reporting the actual numbers (e.g., final returns on each environment, with confidence intervals) or citing a specific table or figure in the appendix, the reader cannot assess the fidelity of the reproduction. This matters because if the base implementation deviates from the original PPO, all subsequent comparisons may be biased. The authors should either (a) include a brief summary of the reproduction results (e.g., 'On Hopper, our implementation achieved 3500±200 vs. 3400±150 in [17]') or (b) cite the exact appendix section and figure where these results are shown.

comments.title: PPO justification is circular for a unified algorithm
comments.quote: We chose PPO because it is probably the most commonly used on-policy deep actor-critic RL algorithm at the moment.
comments.explanation: The justification for using PPO as the reference implementation is its popularity, but this creates a circularity problem for the paper's stated goal of studying 'what matters for on-policy deep actor-critic methods.' If the unified algorithm is anchored to PPO's design choices (e.g., clipping, value function loss), then the study is really about 'what matters within the PPO family,' not about on-policy actor-critic methods in general. For instance, the paper does not include a choice for whether to use a trust-region constraint (as in TRPO) versus a clipped surrogate objective (as in PPO) — that decision is baked into the base implementation. The authors should acknowledge this limitation explicitly and discuss how their findings might change if a different base algorithm (e.g., A2C with KL penalty) were used instead.

comments.title: Base configuration may dominate results
comments.quote: other settings (for choices not in the group) are set to settings of a competitive base configuration (detailed in Appendix C) that is close to the default PPOv2 configuration scaled up to $256$ parallel environments.
comments.explanation: The paper does not justify why this particular base configuration is "competitive" or how it was chosen. If the base configuration is suboptimal for some choices, the results may be biased — a choice that works well with the base configuration may appear important, while one that requires different settings may appear unimportant. The authors should either (a) demonstrate that the base configuration is near-optimal for all groups, or (b) vary the base configuration across experiments to test sensitivity. Without this, the conclusions may be artifacts of the specific base configuration used.

comments.title: Confusion between seeds and configurations
comments.quote: While we take 3 random seeds to compute the performance measure for a single choice configuration, it is important to note that all the experimental results reported in this paper are based on more than 3 random seeds: The reported conditional 95th percentile and distribution of choice within top 5% configurations are computed based upon the performance of hundreds of choice configurations.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that using hundreds of configurations compensates for having only 3 seeds per configuration, but this conflates two different sources of variation. The conditional 95th percentile aggregates across configurations, each with 3 seeds, so the effective sample size for any single configuration remains 3. The variance across configurations reflects differences in hyperparameters, not the stochasticity of the training process for a fixed configuration. To claim robustness against seed variance, one would need to show that the ranking of choices is stable across seeds, not just that many configurations were tested. The argument as written is misleading. Rewrite to clarify that the analysis aggregates across configurations but does not increase the effective number of seeds per configuration, and note that seed variance within a configuration is still only captured by 3 seeds.

comments.title: Confidence intervals do not address seed variance
comments.quote: Furthermore, we also report confidence intervals for the conditional 95th percentile.
comments.explanation: Reporting confidence intervals for the conditional 95th percentile is a good practice, but the text does not specify what these intervals capture. If they are computed via bootstrapping across configurations, they reflect uncertainty in the percentile estimate due to the finite number of configurations, not the uncertainty due to the small number of seeds per configuration. The reader may incorrectly interpret these intervals as addressing the seed-variance concern raised in the first sentence. The paper should explicitly state what source of variability the confidence intervals account for, and acknowledge that they do not mitigate the limitation of using only 3 seeds per configuration.

comments.title: Trust-region claim lacks direct evidence
comments.quote: Overall, these results show that trust-region optimization (preventing the current policy from diverging too much from the behavioral one) which is present in all the other policy losses is crucial for good sample complexity.
comments.explanation: The comparison only shows that PPO, AWR, V-MPO, and RPA outperform PG and V-trace. Since these losses differ in multiple ways (clipping vs. weighting, loss shape, hyperparameters), the observed performance gap cannot be attributed solely to trust-region optimization. To support the claim, the authors would need an ablation study (e.g., removing the clipping from PPO or adding a trust-region penalty to PG). Remove the sentence or qualify it as a hypothesis: 'These results suggest that trust-region optimization may be important for good sample complexity, but further ablation studies are needed to confirm this.'

comments.title: Conjecture conflates trust region and KL regularization
comments.quote: While it is surprising that regularization does not help at all (apart from HalfCheetah), we conjecture that regularization might be less important in our experiments because: (1) the PPO policy loss already enforces the trust region which makes KL penalties or constraints redundant; and (2) the careful policy initialization (See Sec. 3.2) is enough to guarantee good exploration making the entropy bonus or constraint redundant.
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies a logical gap. The PPO clipped surrogate loss enforces a trust region on the change from the previous policy, but the KL regularizers studied include terms that push the policy toward a fixed reference distribution (e.g., N(0,1)) or the behavioral policy, which serve a fundamentally different purpose. The paper does not provide evidence that the PPO loss implicitly achieves the same effect as these specific KL regularizers. The authors should either provide such evidence or revise the conjecture to acknowledge the distinct roles. Rewrite the conjecture to separate the trust-region effect from the reference-distribution regularization, e.g., 'The PPO trust region may reduce the need for KL penalties that constrain policy change, but it does not address regularization toward a fixed reference distribution.'

comments.title: Vague claim about exponential failure probability
comments.quote: If there are many choices, the probability of such failure increases exponentially.
comments.explanation: The claim that failure probability increases exponentially with the number of choices is not justified. The authors do not define what constitutes a 'failure' or provide a model of how choices interact to produce failure. Without specifying a probability distribution over configurations and a threshold for 'no learning,' the statement is too vague to be meaningful. The authors should either remove this claim or replace it with a precise statement, e.g., 'If each of N choices independently has probability p of causing failure, the overall failure probability is 1−(1−p)^N, which grows rapidly with N.'

comments.title: Unclear how implementation decisions became choices
comments.quote: Whenever we were faced with implementation decisions that required us to take decisions that could not be clearly motivated or had alternative solutions, we further added such decisions as additional choices.
comments.explanation: The criterion for adding a choice is subjective and underspecified. 'Could not be clearly motivated' is not a reproducible rule — one researcher's ambiguity is another's settled convention. This introduces a potential selection bias: the set of choices studied may reflect the authors' own uncertainties rather than the full space of design decisions that matter in practice. For example, if the authors were confident about using a particular learning rate schedule, they would not add it as a choice, even if that schedule significantly affects performance. The paper would be stronger if it instead defined a systematic procedure: e.g., 'We included all design decisions that varied across the top-10 most-cited on-policy actor-critic implementations.' Without such grounding, the choice set may be incomplete or idiosyncratic.

comments.title: Vague environment version specification
comments.quote: Hopper-v1, Walker2d-v1, HalfCheetah-v1, Ant-v1, and Humanoid-v1
comments.explanation: The paper specifies environment versions as '-v1' (e.g., Hopper-v1), but the widely used versions in the literature are '-v2' or '-v3' for MuJoCo environments in OpenAI Gym. The '-v1' versions were deprecated and have different reward scales and termination conditions compared to '-v2' and '-v3'. This makes it difficult for readers to reproduce the exact setup. The authors should specify the exact version used and ideally provide the environment source (e.g., Gym version number) to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing justification for environment selection
comments.quote: As benchmark environments, we consider five widely used continuous control environments from OpenAI Gym *[12]* of varying complexity: Hopper-v1, Walker2d-v1, HalfCheetah-v1, Ant-v1, and Humanoid-v1 .
comments.explanation: The paper claims these environments have 'varying complexity' but provides no quantitative or qualitative measure of what complexity means here (e.g., state dimension, action dimension, reward sparsity, horizon length). Without such justification, the claim is vague and the selection appears arbitrary. The authors should either define complexity (e.g., number of degrees of freedom, task difficulty) or remove the phrase. Additionally, all five environments are locomotion tasks with similar state/action spaces, which limits diversity.

comments.title: Inconsistent number of evaluation points across tasks
comments.quote: For each choice configuration, we train $3$ models with independent random seeds where each model is trained for one million (Hopper, HalfCheetah, Walker2d) or two million environment steps (Ant, Humanoid). We evaluate trained policies every hundred thousand steps by freezing the policy and computing the average undiscounted episode return of 100 episodes (with the stochastic policy). We then average these score to obtain a single performance score of the seed which is proportional to the area under the learning curve.
comments.explanation: The number of evaluation points differs (10 for 1M-step tasks, 20 for 2M-step tasks). The claim that the score is 'proportional to the area under the learning curve' holds within each task because the interval length (100k steps) is constant, so the average return times total steps gives the area. However, the scores are not directly comparable across tasks with different total steps because the area scales with total steps. The paper should explicitly note that performance scores are only comparable within tasks of the same training length, or normalize by total steps. Add a sentence after the quoted text: 'Because the number of evaluation points differs across tasks, performance scores are only comparable within tasks with the same total training steps.'

comments.title: Median of three seeds may be unreliable
comments.quote: The performance score of a hyperparameter configuration is finally set to the median performance score across the 3 seeds. This reduces the impact of training noise, i.e., that certain seeds of the same configuration may train much better than others.
comments.explanation: With only 3 seeds, the median is not a robust statistic. For example, scores [0.4, 0.5, 0.9] give median 0.5, while the mean is 0.6. The median does reduce the impact of a single extreme outlier compared to the mean, but with only 3 points, the median is still sensitive to the arrangement of values. The authors should acknowledge this limitation and consider reporting both mean and median, or providing individual seed values in an appendix. Add a sentence: 'With only 3 seeds, the median is not a robust estimator; we report individual seed scores in the appendix for transparency.'

comments.title: Misleading claim about full experimental design and plots
comments.quote: For each group, we provide a full experimental design and full experimental plots in Appendices D - K so that the reader can draw their own conclusions from the experimental results.
comments.explanation: The claim that Appendices D–K contain 'full experimental design and full experimental plots' is contradicted by the paper's own structure. Appendix D (Policy Losses) does not report the exact hyperparameter ranges tested for each loss variant (e.g., clipping epsilon for PPO, KL penalty coefficient for TRPO-style losses). Appendix E (Network Architecture) omits the specific layer widths and activation functions used in the base configuration. Without these details, a reader cannot reproduce the experiments or assess whether the tested configurations are fair. The paper should either include the missing hyperparameter ranges in the appendices or qualify the claim (e.g., 'summaries of experimental designs').

comments.title: Vague recommendation scope limits practical utility
comments.quote: In the following sections, we provide short descriptions of the experiments, our interpretation of the results, as well as practical recommendations for agent training for continuous control.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'practical recommendations for agent training for continuous control' overstates the scope of the study. The experiments are conducted on only five MuJoCo locomotion tasks (Hopper, Walker2d, HalfCheetah, Ant, Humanoid), which share similar state/action spaces and reward structures. The recommendations may not transfer to other continuous control domains such as robotics manipulation, dexterous hand control, or tasks with visual observations. The paper should explicitly restrict the recommendation scope to 'continuous locomotion tasks in MuJoCo' or add a caveat about limited generalizability.

comments.title: Missing raw data accessibility details
comments.quote: Moreover, the raw data from all training runs and a script used to generate all plots for this paper can be found online
comments.explanation: The statement that raw data and plotting scripts are available online is too vague to be actionable. The paper does not provide a URL, repository name, or DOI. Without this information, readers cannot verify the claims or reuse the data. The authors should include a specific link (e.g., GitHub repository) or a footnote with the exact location, as is standard practice in reproducible research.

comments.title: Recommendation inconsistent with observed data
comments.quote: For PPO and its clipping threshold  $\epsilon$  (C16), we further observe that  $\epsilon = 0.2$  and  $\epsilon = 0.3$  perform reasonably well in all environments but that lower  $(\epsilon = 0.1)$  or higher  $(\epsilon = 0.5)$  values give better performance on some of the environments (See Fig. 10 and Fig. 32).
comments.explanation: The text reports that 0.1 and 0.5 give better performance on some environments, yet the recommendation starts at 0.25, which was not explicitly tested. The authors should either justify why 0.25 is a reasonable default (e.g., it is the midpoint of the well-performing range 0.2–0.3) or change the recommendation to start at 0.2 or 0.3, which are directly supported by the data. Rewrite the recommendation as: 'Start with the clipping threshold set to 0.2 or 0.3, but also try lower and higher values if possible.'

comments.title: RPA definition is mathematically ambiguous
comments.quote: and the limiting case of AWR ( $\beta \rightarrow 0$ ) and V-MPO ( $\epsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0$ ) which we call Repeat Positive Advantages (RPA) as it is equivalent to the negative log-probability of actions with positive advantages. See App. B.3
comments.explanation: The phrase 'negative log-probability of actions with positive advantages' could mean either (a) the loss is the negative log-probability for actions with positive advantage and zero for others, or (b) the loss is weighted by the advantage sign. The paper should provide the explicit loss function: e.g., L_RPA(θ) = -Σ_{t: A_t > 0} log π_θ(a_t|s_t). Add a precise mathematical definition in Appendix B.3 to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Scope mismatch: study claims general on-policy insight
comments.quote: The goal of this study is to better understand the importance of the policy loss function in the on-policy deep actor-critic setting considered in this paper.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction claim the study provides general insights for on-policy deep actor-critic methods, but the experiments are limited to 5 environments (Ant, HalfCheetah, Humanoid, etc.) with a specific architecture and hyperparameter ranges. The paper does not verify that the conclusions (e.g., PPO superiority, trust-region importance) hold across different network architectures, action spaces, or reward scales. Add a limitation statement: 'These findings are based on a specific set of environments and hyperparameters; generalizing to other settings requires further validation.'

comments.title: Duplicate choice C52 in enumeration
comments.quote: We consider choices related to the network structure and size (C47, C48, C49, C50, C51, C52, C52), activation functions (C55), and initialization of network weights (C56, C57, C58).
comments.explanation: The list includes C52 twice. This is a typographical error; the second occurrence should likely be C53 or C54. The authors should consult Appendix E to determine the correct choice and correct the list accordingly. Rewrite the list as (C47, C48, C49, C50, C51, C52, C53) or remove the duplicate.

comments.title: Figure 30 referenced for two different claims
comments.quote: As for activation functions, we observe that tanh activations perform best and relu worst. (Fig. 30).
comments.explanation: Figure 30 is cited for both activation function results and minimum action standard deviation results. A single figure cannot support both distinct claims unless it is a multi-panel figure with separate panels for each. The authors should verify the correct figure numbers (likely separate figures for activation functions and for minimum standard deviation) and correct the references to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Missing justification for separate network recommendation
comments.quote: Separate value and policy networks (C47) appear to lead to better performance on four out of five environments (Fig. 15). To avoid analyzing the other choices based on bad models, we thus focus for the rest of this experiment only on agents with separate value and policy networks.
comments.explanation: The paper restricts the remaining analysis to agents with separate networks based on a performance advantage in four of five environments. However, the fifth environment (where separate networks did not perform better) is excluded from further analysis without justification. This selective focus may bias the conclusions toward the separate-network configuration. The authors should either include the fifth environment in the subsequent analysis or explain why it is reasonable to exclude it.

comments.title: V-trace equivalence to N-step in on-policy setting
comments.quote:
GAE and V-trace appear to perform better than N-step returns (Fig. 44 and 40) which indicates that it is beneficial to combine the value estimators from multiple timesteps. We have not

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

found a significant performance difference between GAE and V-trace in our experiments. $\l
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer claims that in an on-policy setting V-trace reduces to N-step returns because importance sampling ratios equal 1. However, V-trace includes a truncation mechanism (clipping of importance weights) that can still produce different estimates even when behavior and target policies are identical, because the truncated weights may not equal 1 if the clipping threshold is less than 1. The paper does not specify the clipping threshold used, so V-trace may indeed differ from N-step. The authors should clarify the V-trace hyperparameters (e.g., clipping threshold) used in their experiments. If clipping was applied, V-trace is not equivalent to N-step and the empirical comparison is valid. If no clipping was used, the claim needs revision. Rewrite the passage to state the V-trace configuration (clipping threshold) and explain why it differs from N-step in the on-policy setting.

comments.title: Recommendation should acknowledge V-trace as alternative
comments.quote:
ecommendation. Use GAE with $\lambda=0.9$ but neither Huber loss nor PPO-style value loss clipping.

##
comments.explanation: The paper states no significant performance difference between GAE and V-trace, yet the recommendation singles out GAE. This is a minor inconsistency. The authors should either explain why GAE is preferred (e.g., simplicity, fewer hyperparameters) or modify the recommendation to include V-trace as an equally viable option. Add a qualifier such as 'either GAE or V-trace with λ=0.9' to align the recommendation with the reported results.

comments.title: Statistical support for λ=0.9 recommendation
comments.quote: .9$ (C8, C9) performed well regardless of whether GAE (Fig. 45) or V-trace (Fig. 49) was used on all tasks but tuning this value per environment may lead to modest performance gains. We h
comments.explanation: The paper's claim that λ=0.9 'performed well' is qualitative and lacks quantitative evidence such as confidence intervals or statistical tests. The authors should either provide statistical comparisons (e.g., mean and standard error across runs) or soften the recommendation to indicate that λ=0.9 is a reasonable default but not necessarily optimal. Add a sentence like 'While λ=0.9 gave strong average performance across tasks, we did not test for statistical significance against other λ values.'

comments.title: Missing justification for combining multiple timesteps
comments.quote: GAE and V-trace appear to perform better than N-step returns (Fig. 44 and 40) which indicates that it is beneficial to combine the value estimators from multiple timesteps.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes the superior performance of GAE and V-trace to combining value estimators from multiple timesteps, but this is not the only difference. N-step returns also combine multiple timesteps (they use n steps). The key difference is that GAE and V-trace use a weighted combination of many n-step returns (exponentially decaying weights), whereas N-step uses a single n-step return. The authors should clarify that the benefit comes from using a weighted average of returns over many horizons, not merely from combining multiple timesteps. Rewrite the sentence to: '...which indicates that it is beneficial to use a weighted combination of returns from multiple horizons, rather than a single n-step return.'

comments.title: C2 and C4 coupling not controlled
comments.quote: Increasing the batch size (C4) does not appear to hurt the sample complexity in the range we tested (Fig. 57) which suggests that it should be increased for faster iteration speed. On the other hand, the number of transitions gathered in each iteration (C2) influences the performance quite significantly (Fig. 52).
comments.explanation: C2 and C4 are coupled through the number of gradient steps per iteration (C2/C4). The paper does not control for this coupling, so the claim that C4 has no effect while C2 has a large effect may be an artifact of the tested ranges. The authors should either explicitly control for the number of gradient steps or discuss this interaction. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'Increasing the batch size (C4) does not appear to hurt the sample complexity in the range we tested (Fig. 57), but this effect may be coupled with the number of transitions per iteration (C2) because the ratio C2/C4 determines the number of gradient steps. Further analysis controlling for this coupling is needed.'

comments.title: Statistical support missing for advantage recomputation claim
comments.quote: The plots suggest that stale advantages can in fact hurt performance and that recomputing them at the beginning of each pass at least partially mitigates the problem and performs best among all variants.
comments.explanation: The claim relies on visual inspection of Figs. 53 and 58 without statistical tests. Overlapping error bars could undermine the conclusion. The authors should report effect sizes or perform a statistical test to justify the recommendation. Add a statistical comparison (e.g., paired t-tests or confidence intervals) between the advantage recomputation variant and the others, and report the results in the text.

comments.title: Advantage recomputation cost not discussed
comments.quote: As a remedy to this problem, we propose to recompute the advantages at the beginning of each pass over the data instead of just once per iteration.
comments.explanation: The paper proposes recomputing advantages at each pass but does not discuss the computational cost. Recomputing advantages requires additional forward passes through the value network, which may be expensive, especially with many passes. The recommendation should address this trade-off. Add a discussion of the computational overhead of advantage recomputation and its impact on wall-clock time, especially when using many passes.

comments.title: Recommendation on frame skip overstates evidence
comments.quote:
all environments. Skipping every other frame (C21) improves the performance on $2$ out of $5$ environments (Fig. 61). Proper handling of episodes abandoned due to the timestep limit seems not to affect the performance (C22, Fig. 62) which

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

is probably caused by the fact that the timestep limit is quite high ($1000$ transitions) in all the environments we considered.

Recommendation. Discount factor $\gamma$ is one of the most important hyperparameters and should be tuned per environment (start with $\gamma=0.99$). Try frame skip if possible.
comments.explanation: The recommendation "Try frame skip if possible" is not well-supported by the evidence: frame skip improves performance on only 2 out of 5 environments, meaning it may hurt or not help on the majority. The phrasing implies a general positive recommendation, which overstates the mixed results. Rewrite the recommendation as: "Frame skip may help in some environments but should be tested per environment; consider it as a candidate hyperparameter."

comments.title: Causal explanation for C22 null result is speculative
comments.quote:
roper handling of episodes abandoned due to the timestep limit seems not to affect the performance (C22, Fig. 62) which

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

is probably caused by the fact that the timestep limit is quite high ($1000$ transitions) in all the environments we considered.

R
comments.explanation: Attributing the null effect of C22 to the high timestep limit is a post-hoc speculation without supporting evidence. The authors do not test lower timestep limits or provide theoretical justification. The phrase "probably caused by" is not a rigorous conclusion from the experimental design. Rewrite the sentence as: "This null effect may be due to the high timestep limit (1000 transitions) in all environments, but further experiments with lower limits would be needed to confirm this hypothesis."

comments.title: Missing justification for discount factor tuning advice
comments.quote: scount factor $\gamma$ is one of the most important hyperparameters and should be tuned per environment (start with $\gamma=0.99$). Try
comments.explanation: The recommendation to tune the discount factor per environment is sensible, but the paper does not provide any theoretical or empirical justification for starting with γ=0.99. The text only states that γ=0.99 "performs reasonably well in all environments," which is a weak basis for a universal starting point. The authors should either cite prior work supporting this default or present a sensitivity analysis showing that 0.99 is a safe default across a wider range of environments. Add a brief justification or reference after the recommendation.

comments.title: Misattribution of Tucker et al. findings
comments.quote: Tucker et al. *[26]* showed that the gains, which had been attributed to one of the recently proposed policy gradients improvements, were, in fact, caused by the implementation details.
comments.explanation: This claim is imprecise and potentially misleading. Tucker et al. (2018) specifically analyzed the PPO clipped surrogate objective and showed that its performance gains over TRPO were largely due to code-level implementation details (e.g., value function clipping, minibatch updates) rather than the clipping mechanism itself. However, the phrase 'one of the recently proposed policy gradients improvements' is vague—it could refer to PPO, but the paper also discusses ACER and other methods. The authors should specify which improvement is being referenced (PPO) and clarify that Tucker et al. attributed the gains to implementation details, not that the improvement itself was entirely an artifact. The current wording risks overstating the finding.

comments.title: Overstated similarity to Engstrom et al.
comments.quote:
The most closely related work to ours is probably Engstrom et al. *[27]* where the authors investigate code-level improvements in the PPO *[17]* code base and conclude that they are responsible for the most of the performance difference between PPO

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

and TRPO *[10]*. Ou
comments.explanation: While Engstrom et al. (2020) is indeed closely related, the claim that their work is 'the most closely related' may be an overstatement. The present paper conducts a large-scale study of over 50 design choices across multiple algorithmic components, whereas Engstrom et al. focused narrowly on code-level implementation details in the PPO/TRPO codebase. The scope and methodology differ substantially—Engstrom et al. did not systematically vary architectural choices, loss functions, or regularization techniques as this paper does. The authors should acknowledge this difference in scope to avoid implying a closer alignment than exists. Additionally, the phrase 'the most of the performance difference' is informal; consider 'a substantial portion' or 'much of'.

comments.title: Missing citation for key related work on reproducibility
comments.quote: Islam et al. *[15]* and Henderson et al. *[22]* point out reproducibility issues in RL including the performance differences between different code bases, the importance of hyperparameter tuning and the high level of stochasticity due to random seeds.
comments.explanation: This statement accurately summarizes the contributions of Islam et al. (2017) and Henderson et al. (2018). However, the paper does not cite the more recent and directly relevant work by Engstrom et al. (2020) in this sentence, even though it is cited later. Given that Engstrom et al. specifically dissected PPO/TRPO implementation details—the very focus of this paper—it would be appropriate to mention it here as a key reproducibility study. The current ordering gives the impression that the reproducibility literature ends with Henderson et al., which is not the case. Consider restructuring to include Engstrom et al. in this initial discussion.

comments.title: Typo in paper outline
comments.quote: The rest of of this paper is structured as follows:
comments.explanation: The phrase 'of of' is a typographical error. It should read 'The rest of this paper is structured as follows:'. While minor, such errors in a published paper detract from professionalism and should be corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguous averaging of evaluation scores
comments.quote: We evaluate trained policies every hundred thousand steps by freezing the policy and computing the average undiscounted episode return of 100 episodes (with the stochastic policy). We then average these score to obtain a single performance score of the seed which is proportional to the area under the learning curve.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'average these score' is ambiguous. The intended meaning is to average the evaluation returns across all evaluation points. Since evaluations are equally spaced every 100k steps, the average of these returns is indeed proportional to the area under the learning curve (the integral of return over steps, approximated by a Riemann sum with equal intervals). The text should clarify that the average is taken over all evaluation points. Rewrite as: 'We then average these evaluation scores across all evaluation points to obtain a single performance score for the seed, which is proportional to the area under the learning curve because the evaluations are equally spaced.'

comments.title: Unsupported claim about interaction effects
comments.quote: Second, many choices may have strong interactions with other related choices, for example the learning rate and the minibatch size. This means that such choices need to be tuned together and experiments where only a single choice is varied but interacting choices are kept fixed may lead to misleading conclusions.
comments.explanation: The claim that learning rate and minibatch size interact strongly is presented as fact without citation or evidence. While such interactions are plausible, the paper's own experimental design (Section 2) varies choices in groups and acknowledges this limitation. The text here should either cite prior work demonstrating this interaction or soften the claim to 'may interact' and note that the paper's grouped design partially addresses this concern. As written, it overstates the certainty of the interaction.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a large-scale empirical study of implementation details in on-policy deep actor-critic methods, training over 250,000 agents across five continuous control environments. The work is valuable for addressing reproducibility issues in deep reinforcement learning, but the experimental design has significant limitations regarding hyperparameter interactions and the scope of recommendations.

The paper makes a commendable effort to standardize and systematically evaluate implementation choices, identifying several surprising and practical insights, such as the importance of policy initialization. However, the research design suffers from a critical flaw: the experimental groups are constructed around thematic categories (e.g., network architecture) rather than systematically exploring interactions between choices. This means the paper cannot determine which combinations of choices work best, and its recommendations may not generalize to other settings where interactions differ. Additionally, the focus on final performance ignores the trade-off between sample efficiency and wall-clock time, which is a key practical concern for practitioners.

Overview issues:

- Experimental design fails to address hyperparameter interactions: The paper groups choices by thematic category (e.g., 'Networks architecture', 'Normalization and clipping') and varies only the choices within each group while keeping others fixed to a 'base configuration' (Section 7). This approach is fundamentally flawed because it cannot detect or account for interactions between choices. For example, the optimal learning rate may depend on the choice of optimizer, the number of parallel environments, and the normalization scheme. By fixing these interacting choices to a single value, the paper may miss the best-performing combinations and its conclusions about the importance of individual choices may be misleading. A proper study would need to use a more systematic search (e.g., random search or Bayesian optimization) across the full space of choices to identify the best configurations and understand how choices interact. This is a major omission that undermines the paper's core contribution of providing practical recommendations.

- Recommendations lack generalizability due to fixed interaction points: The paper's practical recommendations (e.g., 'Use Adam optimizer with momentum β1=0.9', 'Initialize the last policy layer with 100× smaller weights') are derived from experiments where interacting choices were held constant. This means these recommendations are only valid under the specific, fixed context of the paper's base configuration. For instance, the recommendation to use a specific learning rate may only be optimal when combined with the paper's chosen number of parallel environments and minibatch size. If a practitioner changes any of these interacting choices, the recommended hyperparameter may no longer be optimal, potentially leading to poor performance. The paper should acknowledge this limitation and either provide a more comprehensive set of recommendations that account for different contexts or suggest a method for finding the best hyperparameters given a specific set of other choices.

- Performance metric ignores sample efficiency and wall-clock time: The paper's primary performance metric is the area under the learning curve, which rewards agents that learn quickly and achieve high final performance. However, this metric does not account for the computational cost of training, which is a critical factor for practitioners. Some design choices may significantly improve sample efficiency (learning faster in terms of environment steps) but increase wall-clock time due to overhead (e.g., more parallel environments, recomputing advantages). Others may have the opposite effect. By focusing solely on the area under the curve, the paper cannot distinguish between these trade-offs. For example, using many parallel environments (Section 3.5) is shown to decrease sample complexity but may increase wall-clock time if sufficient CPU cores are not available. The paper should include a secondary metric, such as wall-clock time to reach a certain performance threshold, to provide a more complete picture of the practical implications of different choices.

- Limited scope of environments and tasks: The paper evaluates its recommendations across only five continuous control environments from OpenAI Gym: Hopper-v1, Walker2d-v1, HalfCheetah-v1, Ant-v1, and Humanoid-v1. While these are standard benchmarks, they share a common structure (low-dimensional state spaces, simple observation spaces, continuous actions). This limited scope raises questions about the generalizability of the findings. For instance, the importance of policy initialization (Section 3.2) might be specific to these environments or to the particular type of action distributions they use. The paper should acknowledge this limitation and discuss whether the findings are likely to hold for more complex tasks, such as those with high-dimensional visual observations, discrete action spaces, or hierarchical structures. A more diverse set of environments would strengthen the paper's claims about the universality of its recommendations.

- Missing demonstration of result bite: The paper claims to provide practical insights but never demonstrates that a specific, poorly configured agent fails to learn or performs significantly worse than a well-configured one. For a study of implementation choices, it is essential to show that random or naive settings lead to failure. The authors should run a baseline experiment where the policy is initialized with a standard deviation of 1.0 (as is common in many implementations) and observe the learning curve on the Ant or Humanoid environments to quantify the performance gap described in Section 3.2. This would prove the 'bite' of the finding that initial action distribution matters.

- Underdeveloped implications for high-dimensional tasks: The paper focuses on low-dimensional continuous control tasks (Hopper, Ant, etc.) and recommends specific architectural choices like using tanh activations and wide value networks. However, these recommendations may not generalize to high-dimensional visual observations, where ReLU activations are standard and normalization techniques differ. The paper should explicitly analyze or discuss whether the findings regarding network architecture and initialization hold when the observation space is high-dimensional, such as in the Atari or Roboschool environments, or at least acknowledge this limitation and suggest how practitioners might adapt the recommendations.

- Missing comparison to existing approaches: The paper claims to generalize prior work and provide insights, but it never formally compares its unified implementation's performance against the original implementations of the algorithms it studies (e.g., the original PPO, V-trace, or GAE papers). While the authors verify their implementation matches the PPO paper's results (Section 5), they do not verify that their unified agent matches the performance of the specific codebases referenced in the related work (e.g., OpenAI Baselines or SEED RL). To position the paper correctly, the authors should include a section or appendix where they train agents using the original codebases on the same environments and compare the final performance and learning curves to their unified agent to ensure no regressions have occurred.

- Missing implementation guidance for practitioners: The paper provides practical recommendations based on the study but offers no guidance on how to implement these choices in code. For example, it recommends 'adding a (negative) offset to its input to decrease the initial standard deviation of actions' (Section 3.2), but does not explain *how* to compute this offset or where in the codebase to apply it. Similarly, the recommendation to 'recompute the advantages at the beginning of each pass' (Section 3.5) lacks implementation details. To make the paper truly useful for practitioners, the authors should provide pseudo-code or a clear explanation of the implementation details for the most critical recommendations, such as the policy initialization trick and the advantage recomputation strategy.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Re-design the experimental approach to systematically explore interactions between hyperparameters, for example by using random search or Bayesian optimization across the full space of choices, rather than grouping by thematic category.

- Include a secondary performance metric, such as wall-clock time to reach a target performance, to characterize the trade-off between sample efficiency and computational cost.

- Expand the scope of the study to include more diverse environments, such as those with visual observations or discrete action spaces, to assess the generalizability of the findings.

- Provide a more nuanced discussion of the limitations of the current recommendations, explicitly stating which choices must be held constant for a given recommendation to be valid.

comments.title: Binomial approximation is mathematically incorrect
comments.quote: We report the 95th percentile of the performance as well as a confidence interval based on a binomial approximation . Intuitively, this corresponds to a robust estimate of the performance one can expect if all other choices in the group were tuned with random search and a limited budget of roughly 20 hyperparameter configurations.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to compute confidence intervals for the 95th percentile using a binomial approximation. This is mathematically incorrect. The binomial distribution models counts of successes in a fixed number of Bernoulli trials, not the distribution of a percentile of a continuous performance distribution. To estimate uncertainty around a percentile, one should use a bootstrap or order-statistic method. Please correct this statistical claim.

comments.title: Misattribution of self-driving application to TD3
comments.quote: For continuous control tasks, on-policy algorithms such as REINFORCE *[2]*, TRPO *[10]*, A3C *[14]*, PPO *[17]* and off-policy algorithms such as DDPG *[13]* and SAC *[21]* have enabled successful applications such as quadrupedal locomotion *[20]*, self-driving *[30]* or dexterous in-hand manipulation *[20, 25, 32]*.
comments.explanation: The introduction cites [30] as enabling self-driving applications, but [30] is 'Addressing Function Approximation Error in Actor-Critic Methods' (TD3), which does not make that claim. Similarly, [20] is 'Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning' (A3C), not the locomotion paper. Please correct these citations to match the actual papers.

comments.title: Circular definition of on-policy learning
comments.quote: We define on-policy learning in the following loose sense: We consider policy iteration algorithms that iterate between generating experience using the current policy and using that experience to improve the policy.
comments.explanation: The definition of on-policy learning provided is circular: 'iterate between generating experience using the current policy and using that experience to improve the policy' applies to both on-policy and off-policy methods. A clear distinction is needed to explain why the authors' setting differs from off-policy methods like DQN, particularly regarding the use of replay buffers or multiple updates per iteration.

comments.title: Misleading probabilistic argument about failure
comments.quote: If there are many choices, the probability of such failure increases exponentially.
comments.explanation: The statement that failure probability increases exponentially with the number of choices is imprecise. The probability of failure is $1 - 	ext{product}(1 - p_i)$, which depends on the distribution of choices, not simply the count. Please clarify this argument or provide a more rigorous justification.

comments.title: Misleading claim about RPA equivalence
comments.quote: the limiting case of AWR ( $\beta \rightarrow 0$ ) and V-MPO ( $\epsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0$ ) which we call Repeat Positive Advantages (RPA) as it is equivalent to the negative log-probability of actions with positive advantages
comments.explanation: The claim that RPA is equivalent to the negative log-probability of actions with positive advantages is incorrect. RPA retains the weighting of advantages but reduces the influence of negative ones; it does not discard them entirely. Please rewrite this sentence to state that RPA emphasizes positive advantages while reducing the influence of negative ones.

comments.title: Confusing explanation of minibatch handling
comments.quote: The original PPO implementation splits the data in each policy iteration step into individual transitions and then randomly assigns them to minibatches (C5). This makes it impossible to compute advantages as the temporal structure is broken.
comments.explanation: The text claims that splitting data into individual transitions 'makes it impossible to compute advantages as the temporal structure is broken.' This is misleading. GAE relies on temporal difference errors computed from trajectories; shuffling breaks the order for the update but does not prevent advantage computation. Please clarify this distinction.

comments.title: Overstated scope of recommendations
comments.quote: Based on more than 250’000 experiments in five continuous control environments, we evaluate the impact of different choices and provide practical recommendations.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to provide 'practical recommendations' based on a broad study, but the scope is limited to five low-dimensional continuous control environments. The authors do not demonstrate whether these recommendations generalize to high-dimensional visual observations or discrete action spaces. Please acknowledge this limitation or provide evidence for generalizability.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Tucker et al. findings
comments.quote: Tucker et al. *[26]* showed that the gains, which had been attributed to one of the recently proposed policy gradients improvements, were, in fact, caused by the implementation details.
comments.explanation: The related work section mischaracterizes the findings of Tucker et al. [26]. Tucker et al. showed the performance gap was due to implementation details (optimizer/learning rate) in the Baselines codebase, not just algorithmic differences. Please correct this attribution.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Engstrom et al. findings
comments.quote:
The most closely related work to ours is probably Engstrom et al. *[27]* where the authors investigate code-level improvements in the PPO *[17]* code base and conclude that they are responsible for the most of the performance difference between PPO

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

and TRPO *[10]*. Ou
comments.explanation: The statement that Engstrom et al. [27] concluded that code-level improvements were responsible for the performance difference between PPO and TRPO is incorrect. Engstrom et al. showed the gap was due to implementation details in the Baselines PPO codebase being TRPO-like. Please correct this sentence to accurately reflect that Engstrom et al. showed the gap was due to implementation details.

comments.title: Recommendation on episode termination is too strong
comments.quote:
frame skip if possible. There is no need to handle environments step limits in a special way for large step limits.

###
comments.explanation: The recommendation 'There is no need to handle environments step limits in a special way for large step limits' is too strong. The conclusion is based on a single timestep limit (1000). If the limit were lower, handling abandoned episodes could significantly impact performance. Please qualify this recommendation or provide results for a range of limits.

comments.title: Missing enumeration of implemented choices
comments.quote: As a step towards filling that gap, we implement >50 such “choices” in a unified on-policy deep actor-critic framework, allowing us to investigate their impact in a large-scale empirical study.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims to implement '>50 such choices' but the methodology section does not explicitly list or categorize these choices. To improve clarity, add a sentence in the methodology section that explicitly states the total number of choices implemented and provides a brief categorization.

comments.title: Unclear confidence interval method
comments.quote: Furthermore, we also report confidence intervals for the conditional 95th percentile.
comments.explanation: The text states that confidence intervals are reported for the conditional 95th percentile but does not specify the method used to compute them. Readers will wonder whether these intervals are based on the standard error of the mean across seeds, bootstrap resampling, or a different statistical approach. Add a sentence specifying the method.

comments.title: Missing reproducibility details
comments.quote: Moreover, the raw data from all training runs and a script used to generate all plots for this paper can be found online $^{6}$ .
comments.explanation: While providing access to raw data and scripts is commendable, the statement lacks specificity about the exact software versions, dependencies, and configuration files required to reproduce the experiments. To ensure true reproducibility, the authors should specify the exact software versions and command-line arguments used for each run.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to other fields
comments.quote: Our work is also similar to other large-scale studies done in other fields of Deep Learning, e.g. model-based RL *[31]*, GANs *[24]*, NLP *[35]*, disentangled representations *[23]* and convolution network architectures *[36]*.
comments.explanation: The comparison of this RL study to large-scale studies in other fields like GANs and NLP is misleading. While the paper uses a large number of random seeds and configurations, the nature of the experiments is fundamentally different (hyperparameter tuning vs. scaling laws). Please clarify the distinction between their study and other large-scale studies.
